6: simeini kachotam al libecha kachotam al zeroa'echa ki azza kamavet ahavah qasha kishol qina'ah reshefeyha reshefei esh shalhevetyah: 'Set me as a seal upon your heart, as a seal upon your arm, for love is strong as death, jealousy is fierce as the grave. Its flashes are flashes of fire, the very flame of the LORD.' The climax of the Song's celebration of love: it is as strong as death and as fierce as sheol — the two most powerful forces in human experience. Shalhevetyah (the very flame of the LORD) — uniquely, this is one of the few places in the Song where the divine name appears, even embedded in a word. The NT's fulfillment: the love of Christ has defeated death (1 Cor 15:54-57) and nothing can separate us from that love (Rom 8:38-39); what the Song claimed about love's unconquerability is literally true in Christ's resurrection.
1: mi yittencha ke'ach li: ‘Who will give you to me as a brother’ — the use of mi yitten (who will give, the Hebrew idiom for “would that!” or “if only”) expresses wish for something currently impossible. The social context: in the ancient Near East, a woman could kiss a brother in public without social reproach, but kissing a non-family male in the street would result in her being buz-ed (contemned, despised, H936) — subjected to the social shame of breaking the honor code governing public male-female contact. The beloved’s wish is not that the beloved were literally her sibling but that the intimacy of sibling affection could be publicly expressed without social jeopardy. The verse is a precise social observation: love creates longings for a freedom of expression that social conventions restrict.
2: 'avi-me: The mother’s house (beit immi, H517, my mother’s house) appears three times in the Song (3:4, 8:2) as the primary social sanctuary for the beloved — the place of legitimate reception of the beloved. The instruction the beloved would receive from her mother — in the context of the spiced wine and pomegranate nectar she would serve — reflects the role of the mother in the ancient Near East as the transmitter of domestic wisdom, particularly regarding the preparation of the household for the relationship of marriage. Rimmon (H7416, pomegranate) is a recurring image in the Song for the beloved’s beauty (4:3, 6:7, 7:12) and here for the gift she would offer the beloved. The pomegranate was the ANE symbol of fertility and abundance; its juice as the gift of the beloved’s hospitality elevates the natural fruit to the level of love-gift.
3: The left-hand/right-hand embrace — semalo tachat roshi ve-yamino techabbakeni (his left hand under my head and his right hand embraces me) — repeats 2:6 verbatim. The repetition within the Song functions as a structural echo: the reclining tenderness of 2:6 and 8:3 frames the entire middle section of the book (chs. 3-7) with the same image of covenantal closeness. The left hand under the head is the posture of the protective reclining embrace — the protective cradle; the right hand embracing is the encircling hold. Together they describe the full-body embrace of the beloved by the one who holds her.
4: The daughters of Jerusalem adjuration (hishba'ti etkem… mah-ta'iru umah-te'oreru et-ha'ahavah, I adjure you… why would you stir up and awaken love) is the Song’s recurring refrain (2:7, 3:5). The gazelle/deer invocation present in 2:7 and 3:5 is absent here, making this the more compressed form of the adjuration. The refrain’s placement at the beginning of the final chapter functions as a summary: having traced the entire arc of love’s seeking, finding, losing, and re-finding, the beloved reissues the counsel of patient waiting. Love that is authentic cannot be forced, manufactured, or artificially stirred up; it arrives in its own time and must be awaited rather than provoked.
5: mecholeliteka: Mi zo't olah min-hamidbar mitrapeket al-dodah (who is this coming up from the wilderness, leaning on her beloved?) — the procession image (cf. 3:6) recurs here, but now the figure is identified by her intimacy: she leans on (mitrapeket, clinging, leaning upon) her beloved. The final wilderness-to-Zion procession image of the Song has been transformed from the perfumed-smoke procession of ch3 (which featured Solomon’s couch, cedar wood, and sixty warriors) into an intimate pair. Mecholeliteka (H2342, gave birth to you, from chul, to writhe/dance/give birth) — the same root used for birth pangs throughout the prophets (Isa 26:17, Jer 4:31, Mic 4:10) and for the trembling of creation. The apple tree (cf. 2:3 where the bridegroom is an apple tree for the beloved) as the birthplace creates a structural inclusion: the tree under which the beloved rested (2:3) is now the tree under which the beloved was born. Love has the quality of rebirth at its origin point.
7: buz yevuzu lo: The continuation of v6’s declaration — mayim rabbim lo yukelu lechabbot et-ha'ahavah (many waters are not able to quench the love) and rivers (neharot) cannot drown it. The many waters and rivers are images of the ANE flood-chaos (cf. Ps 93:3-4 where the floods/rivers roar against YHWH’s throne; Ps 46:2-3 where mountains fall into the sea and waters rage). Love that the chaos-waters cannot extinguish is love that participates in YHWH’s own stability against the chaotic deep. The wealth-contempt coda: im yitten ish et-kol-hon beito ba'ahavah boz yevuzu lo (if a man were to give all the wealth of his house for love, he would be utterly despised). Buz (H936, contempt/treating with contempt) — the social shaming of the one who attempts to commodify love through commercial exchange. The attempt to purchase what can only be freely given is not merely unsuccessful but self-disgracing: it places the buyer in the position of the one who fundamentally misunderstands the nature of the thing he seeks.
8: The brothers’ deliberation about the little sister (achot lanu qetannah, we have a little sister) who does not yet have breasts — a younger sibling not yet at marriageable age. The brothers’ question: what will we do for our sister on the day she is spoken for? The protective role of the brothers toward their sister’s honor was a social norm in ancient Israel (cf. Gen 34:25-29 where Simeon and Levi avenge Dinah’s honor). The question shows that the beloved’s brothers have been thinking about her future: the two possibilities (wall or door) represent two different assessments of her character and two corresponding responses.
9: The wall/door alternatives: im chomah hi (if she is a wall, H2346) the brothers would build upon her a turret of silver (tira'at kesef, H2918 + H3701); ve-im delet hi (if she is a door, H1817) they would enclose her with cedar boards (luch erez, H3871 + H730). The wall represents the impenetrable — a woman whose character is like a fortified wall, whose integrity needs only to be adorned and honored; the door represents the open passage that needs to be secured. The distinction is not judgmental but architectural: the brothers respond to what the beloved is with the appropriate protection. Silver turrets ornament what is already strong; cedar boards secure what is vulnerable. The verse anticipates the beloved’s own declaration in v10.
10: shalom / shlomoh: Ani chomah u-shadai kamigdalot az hayiti be'einav kemotze'et shalom (I am a wall, and my breasts are like towers; thus I have become in his eyes as one who finds peace). The beloved answers the brothers’ hypothetical with a declaration: she is the wall, not the door — her integrity is the fortress. The towers of her body adorning what was already strong confirms the silver-turret scenario of v9. Shalom (H7965, peace/wholeness/completeness) as the beloved’s self-description creates a deliberate wordplay with shlomoh (Solomon, H8010, the peaceful one). The beloved who is a wall becomes in the bridegroom’s eyes the one who finds/gives shalom — she mirrors the Solomon-quality that the Song has associated with the bridegroom throughout. The verse’s wordplay: the beloved-as-wall achieves in her integrity the shalom that the Solomon-figure embodies.
11: ba'al hamon / kerem: Solomon’s vineyard at Ba'al Hamon (H1174, master of abundance or Baal-Hamon, an unidentified location) follows the vineyard-ownership theme from 1:6. The vineyard lets out to keepers (notrim, H5201, watchmen/guardians — the same root used for keeping the law and for the vineyard-keepers in Isa 5:1-7) for a rent of a thousand silver pieces (elef kesef). The commercial vineyard, rented and productive for profit, is the literary backdrop against which the beloved makes her declaration in v12.
12: karmi shelli lefanai: Karmi shelli lefanai (my own vineyard is before me, belonging to me) — the emphatic shelli (mine, my own) is the rhetorical climax of the vineyard imagery that has run through the Song from 1:6 (where the beloved lamented she had not kept her own vineyard while keeping others’). Here the beloved asserts that her vineyard — her own self and her love — is hers to give, not to rent for silver. The thousand pieces for Solomon and the two hundred for the keepers (la-notrim) contrast with the priceless self-gift the beloved offers freely. The commercial transaction of the vineyard-rent is implicitly contrasted with v7’s statement that love cannot be purchased: the beloved’s vineyard is free in the sense of both liberty (it is hers to give) and of freely given (it is offered without price).
13: chomeret / yashvu / hashme'ini: The companions (chaverim, H2270) listening for the beloved’s voice creates a scene of communal anticipation: the beloved’s speech is sought not only by the bridegroom but by the surrounding community. The chaverim (friends/companions) are a presupposed community of listeners, the circle of relationship that the love-pair exists within. Hashme'ini et-qolekh (let me hear your voice) — the same formula as 2:14 where the bridegroom in the cleft of the rock begs to hear the beloved’s voice. The voice of the beloved that the companions gather to hear and that the bridegroom specifically requests is the most intimate gift of the whole person: the beloved’s words are what she gives most fully, beyond the gifts of the vineyard and the mandrakes.
14: berach dodi: The Song’s final line — berach dodi vedameh lekha litsvi o le'ofer ha'ayyalim al-harim vessamim (flee, my beloved, and be like a gazelle or a young stag on the mountains of spices) — is not a permanent farewell but the invitation to return. Berach (H1272, to flee/move through quickly) is the verb for the swift movement of animals through landscape — the same root used for the patriarchs’ flight and for the movement of the divine spirit. The gazelle/stag imagery (repeated from 2:9, 2:17) frames the ending as open, dynamic movement rather than static arrival: the Song ends with the beloved in motion, racing over the fragrant mountains, with the beloved calling after him. The mountains of spices (harim vessamim) — not a specific location but the entire fragrant landscape of the Song — are where the beloved runs. The ending is the beginning of the next seeking.
